subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).

Assertion (A): The first ionization enthalpy of 3d series elements is more than that of group 2 metals

Reason (R): In 3d series of elements successive filling of d-orbitals takes place.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).

Assertion (A): The first ionization enthalpy of 3d series elements is more than that of group 2 metals

Reason (R): In 3d series of elements successive filling of d-orbitals takes place.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
options: Both (A) and (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation of (A)
Both (A) and (R) are true but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A)
solution:
Metal Sc Ti V Cr Mn Fe Co Ni Cu Zn
IE (kJ/mol) 631 656 650 653 717 762 758 736 745 906
 
Metal Be Mg Ca Sr Ba Ra
IE (kJ/mol) 899 737 590 549 503 509

In the first transition series, the first and second ionization enthalpy values increase slowly on moving across the period. As the nuclear charge rises, the interaction between the nucleus and valence electrons increases, and as a result, the ionization enthalpy increases. So, Sc has the lowest value of ionization enthalpy while Zn has the highest value of ionization enthalpy.

So, both assertion and reasons are true and reason is the correct expalantion of assertion.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
q: The first ionization enthalpies of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Be</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></math> follow the order
question: The first ionization enthalpies of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Be</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></math> follow the order
solution:
The ionisation enthalpy increases as we go from left to right in a period, while it decreases as we come down a group. So according to this, the order will be Be<B<C<N<O. But there are two exceptions, in the case of Be and N because of the highly stable 2s2 (completely filled orbital) and 2s22p3 (half-filled orbital 2p3) valence configuration. Due to this, the correct order is:
B<Be<C<O<N.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
q:
Given below are the two statements: one is labeled as Assertion (A) and the other is labeled as Reason (R).

Assertion (A): There is a considerable increase in covalent radius from N to P. However from As to Bi only a small increase in covalent radius is observed.

Reason (R): covalent and ionic radii in a particular oxidation state increases down the group.

In the light of the above statement, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are the two statements: one is labeled as Assertion (A) and the other is labeled as Reason (R).

Assertion (A): There is a considerable increase in covalent radius from N to P. However from As to Bi only a small increase in covalent radius is observed.

Reason (R): covalent and ionic radii in a particular oxidation state increases down the group.

In the light of the above statement, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
options: Both (A) and (R) are true but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A)
Both (A) and (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation of (A)
solution:
There is a considerable increase in covalent radius from N to P. However, from As to Bi only a small increase in covalent radius is observed. This is due to the presence of completely filled d and/or f orbitals in heavier members.

As going down in a group, the number of shells increases, hence the covalent and ionic radii increases.

Both (A) and (R) are true but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A)

But the correct explanation is the presence of completely filled d and f-orbitals of heavier members.

q: Lowest Oxidation number of an atom in a compound <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo></math> The number of an electron in its valence shell is
question: Lowest Oxidation number of an atom in a compound <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo></math> The number of an electron in its valence shell is
solution: A2 B→2 A++B-2, B-2 has complete octet in its dianionic form, thus in its atomic state it has 6 electrons in its valence shell. As it has negative charge, it has acquired two electrons to complete its octet. If the lowest oxidation state of an atom is -2, this typically implies that the atom can gain two electrons to reach a stable configuration. Therefore, the number of valence electrons in that atom would be six, because gaining two electrons would fill its outer shell.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
q: The correct trend in the first ionization enthalpies of the elements in the $3^{\text {rd }}$ period of periodic table is :
question: The correct trend in the first ionization enthalpies of the elements in the $3^{\text {rd }}$ period of periodic table is :
solution:
First ionization enthalpy generally increases across a period due to increasing nuclear charge and decreasing atomic radius. However, exceptions occur based on electron configuration stability.
For 3rd period elements, the approximate ionization enthalpy values are: Al (578), Si (787), P (1012), S (1000), Cl (1251) kJ/mol.
(1) Al has lower IE than Si because removing 3p¹ is easier than removing one electron from 3p².
(2) S has slightly lower IE than P despite being to the right because P has a half-filled p-orbital (3p³) which is more stable.
(3) Cl has the highest IE as it approaches a filled p-orbital.
The correct order is: Al < Si < S < P < Cl

q: The ionic radius of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Na</mi><mo>+</mo></msup></math> ions is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>02</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>°</mo></mover><mo>.</mo></math> The ionic radii (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>°</mo></mover></math>) of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Mg</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Al</mi><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> respectively, are
question: The ionic radius of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Na</mi><mo>+</mo></msup></math> ions is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>02</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>°</mo></mover><mo>.</mo></math> The ionic radii (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>°</mo></mover></math>) of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Mg</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Al</mi><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> respectively, are
solution:
As we know nuclear charge increases, attraction of nucleus on electron increases because of that size decreases.

Here all ions have same number of electrons so as the number of protons increases nuclear charge increases and size decreases.

The ionic radii order is Na+>Mg2+>Al3+

q: If IUPAC name of an element is "Unununnium" then the element belongs to nth group of periodic table. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> is _______.
question: If IUPAC name of an element is "Unununnium" then the element belongs to nth group of periodic table. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> is _______.
solution:
Unununnium is the IUPAC name given to the element Roentgenium and the atomic number 111. In the periodic table in the 7th period it is located and placed in the 11th group. Roentgenium is a radioactive element and its isotopes are unstable in nature.

111 belongs to 11th group.

Hence the value of n is 11.

question: Which is the correct order of second ionization potential of $\mathrm{C}, \mathrm{N}, \mathrm{O}$ and $\mathrm{F}$ in the following ?
solution: The second ionization potential means removal of electron from cation $\mathrm{C}^{+}=1 s^2 2 s^2 2 p^1, \mathrm{~N}^{+}=1 s^2 2 s^2 2 p^2$ $\mathrm{O}^{+}=1 s^2 2 s^2 2 p^3, \mathrm{~F}^{+}=1 s^2 2 s^2 2 p^4$ Therefore $\mathrm{O}>\mathrm{F}>\mathrm{N}>\mathrm{C}$

q: Consider the elements $\mathrm{N}, \mathrm{P}, \mathrm{O}, \mathrm{S}, \mathrm{Cl}$ and F. The number of valence electrons present in the elements with most and least metallic character from the above list is respectively.
question: Consider the elements $\mathrm{N}, \mathrm{P}, \mathrm{O}, \mathrm{S}, \mathrm{Cl}$ and F. The number of valence electrons present in the elements with most and least metallic character from the above list is respectively.
solution:
Valence electrons: N(5), P(5), O(6), S(6), Cl(7), F(7).
Metallic character order in the list: P > S > N > Cl > O > F.
Most metallic character: P with 5 valence electrons.
Least metallic character (most nonmetallic): F with 7 valence electrons.
F is the most electronegative and has the smallest atomic size, making it most nonmetallic.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
q:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I : The correct order in terms of atomic/ionic radii is $\mathrm{Al}>\mathrm{Mg}>\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}>\mathrm{Al}^{3+}$.
Statement II : The correct order in terms of the magnitude of electron gain enthalpy is $\mathrm{Cl}>\mathrm{Br}>\mathrm{S}>\mathrm{O}$.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I : The correct order in terms of atomic/ionic radii is $\mathrm{Al}>\mathrm{Mg}>\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}>\mathrm{Al}^{3+}$.
Statement II : The correct order in terms of the magnitude of electron gain enthalpy is $\mathrm{Cl}>\mathrm{Br}>\mathrm{S}>\mathrm{O}$.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
solution:
Analyzing Statement I: The order given is Al > Mg > Mg²⁺ > Al³⁺. However, the correct atomic radii order should be Mg > Al because Mg is in group 2 (period 3) while Al is in group 13 (period 3), and moving right across a period decreases radius. The correct order would be Mg > Al > Mg²⁺ > Al³⁺. Therefore, Statement I is FALSE.

Analyzing Statement II: Electron gain enthalpy magnitudes follow: Cl (highest, ~349 kJ/mol) > Br (~325) > S (~200) > O (~141). This is because Cl has the highest tendency to gain an electron due to its position and electronic configuration. Statement II is TRUE.

Since Statement I is false and Statement II is true, the answer is option (3).

q: Which of the following presents the correct order of second ionization enthalpies of $\mathrm{C}, \mathrm{N}, \mathrm{O}$ and $\mathrm{F}$ ?
question: Which of the following presents the correct order of second ionization enthalpies of $\mathrm{C}, \mathrm{N}, \mathrm{O}$ and $\mathrm{F}$ ?
solution:
In a period the value of ionisation potential increases from left to right with breaks where the atoms have some what stable configuration. After removing an $e^{-}$ oxygen will acquire stable configuration. Hence $\mathrm{O}^{+}$has highest second ionisation enthalpy than $\mathrm{F}^{+}$and $\mathrm{N}^{+}$.
$\therefore$ correct order is $\mathrm{O}>\mathrm{F}>\mathrm{N}>\mathrm{C}$

chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A : The energy required to form Mg2+from Mg is much higher than that required to produce Mg+

Reason R:Mg2+ is small ion and carry more charge than Mg+

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A : The energy required to form Mg2+from Mg is much higher than that required to produce Mg+

Reason R:Mg2+ is small ion and carry more charge than Mg+

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
options: Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
solution:
Mg have 2 electrons in valance shell and after donation of 2 electrons it converts into Mg2+ ion and attains inert gas configuration, now to ionize one more electron from inert gas configuration is very difficult, hence it is easy to ionize Mg to Mg2+.  IE2 is always greater than IE1 because on removal of e-, size decreases and effective nuclear charge increases.So Assertion is correct.

Mg2+ is a smaller ion than Mg+ and has a greater charge density. This means that Mg2+ has a stronger attraction to its electrons than Mg+ and requires more energy to remove an electron from it. So reason is also correct.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
q: Elements X and Y belong to Group 15. The difference between the electronegativity values of ' X ' and phosphorus is higher than that of the difference between phosphorus and ' Y '. ' X ' \& ' Y ' are respectively
question: Elements X and Y belong to Group 15. The difference between the electronegativity values of ' X ' and phosphorus is higher than that of the difference between phosphorus and ' Y '. ' X ' \& ' Y ' are respectively
solution:
Group 15 elements have electronegativity values (Pauling scale): N = 3.04, P = 2.19, As = 2.18, Sb = 2.05, Bi = 2.02.
The condition requires: |EN(X) - EN(P)| > |EN(P) - EN(Y)|.
(1) N and As: |3.04 - 2.19| = 0.85 and |2.19 - 2.18| = 0.01. Since 0.85 > 0.01, the condition is satisfied.
(2) As and Sb: |2.18 - 2.19| = 0.01 and |2.19 - 2.05| = 0.14. Since 0.01 < 0.14, it fails.
(3) Bi and N: |2.02 - 2.19| = 0.17 and |2.19 - 3.04| = 0.85. Since 0.17 < 0.85, it fails.
(4) As and Bi: |2.18 - 2.19| = 0.01 and |2.19 - 2.02| = 0.17. Since 0.01 < 0.17, it fails.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
q: The correct decreasing order for metallic character is
question: The correct decreasing order for metallic character is
solution:
A metal is a material that, when freshly prepared, polished, or fractured, shows a lustrous appearance, and conducts electricity and heat relatively well. Metals are typically malleable (they can be hammered into thin sheets) or ductile (can be drawn into wires)

On moving top to bottom of the periodic table metallic character increases and on moving left to right metallic character decreases.

Metallic character is directly proportional to the atomic size.

The correct order of atomic size of the given elements is Na>Mg>Be>Si>P.

Hence, correct order of metallic character is Na>Mg>Be>Si>P

All these elements are form 3rd period except beryllium which is from second period

Na is an alkali metal

Mg and Be are alkaline earth metals, 

Phosphorous belongs to a group which is commonly called as pnicogens.

Si belongs to 14th group

The oxides of<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Z</mi></math>, respectively, are basic, amphoteric and acidic. The correct order of the atomic numbers of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Z</mi></math> is:
solution:
On moving left to right in a period. Acidic character of oxides is increase. 3rd  period element oxides. Na2O       ⏟Basic MgO  Al2O3⏟Amphoteric   SiO2      P2O5        Cl2O7⏟Acidic 
(i) Acidic character↑
(i) Atomic No↑so X have minimum Atomic No
& Z have maxima Atomic No So correct order is X<Y<Z

chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
q:
Match List-I with List-II.
$\begin{array}{llll} & \text{List-I} & & \text{List-II} \\(A) & \mathrm{Al}^{3+} < \mathrm{Mg}^{2+} < \mathrm{Na}^{+} < \mathrm{F}^{-} & (I) & \text{Ionisation Enthalpy} \\(B) & \mathrm{B} < \mathrm{C} < \mathrm{O} < \mathrm{N} & (II) & \text{Metallic character} \\(C) & \mathrm{B} < \mathrm{Al} < \mathrm{Mg} < \mathrm{K} & (III) & \text{Electronegativity} \\(D) & \mathrm{Si} < \mathrm{P} < \mathrm{S} < \mathrm{Cl} & (IV) & \text{Ionic radii}\end{array}$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Match List-I with List-II.
$\begin{array}{llll} & \text{List-I} & & \text{List-II} \\(A) & \mathrm{Al}^{3+} < \mathrm{Mg}^{2+} < \mathrm{Na}^{+} < \mathrm{F}^{-} & (I) & \text{Ionisation Enthalpy} \\(B) & \mathrm{B} < \mathrm{C} < \mathrm{O} < \mathrm{N} & (II) & \text{Metallic character} \\(C) & \mathrm{B} < \mathrm{Al} < \mathrm{Mg} < \mathrm{K} & (III) & \text{Electronegativity} \\(D) & \mathrm{Si} < \mathrm{P} < \mathrm{S} < \mathrm{Cl} & (IV) & \text{Ionic radii}\end{array}$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
Ionic radii $-\mathrm{Al}^{3+} < \mathrm{Mg}^{2+} < \mathrm{Na}^{+} < \mathrm{F}^{-}$
Ionisation energy - $\mathrm{B} < \mathrm{C} < \mathrm{O} < \mathrm{N}$
Metallic character $-\mathrm{B} < \mathrm{Al} < \mathrm{Mg} < \mathrm{K}$
Electron negativity $-\mathrm{Si} < \mathrm{P} < \mathrm{S} < \mathrm{Cl}$

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
q: Similarity in chemical properties of the atoms of elements in a group of the Periodic table is most closely related to:
question: Similarity in chemical properties of the atoms of elements in a group of the Periodic table is most closely related to:
solution: If elements are arranged in order of their increasing atomic numbers, element coming at intervals of $2,8,8,18,18,32$ and 32 will have similar physical and chemical properties and thus grouped in one particular group.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
q:
The correct statements from the following are :
(A) $\mathrm{Tl}^{3+}$ is a powerful oxidising agent
(B) $\mathrm{Al}^{3+}$ does not get reduced easily
(C) Both $\mathrm{Al}^{3+}$ and $\mathrm{Tl}^{3+}$ are very stable in solution
(D) $\mathrm{Tl}^{+}$is more stable than $\mathrm{Tl}^{3+}$
(E) $\mathrm{Al}^{3+}$ and $\mathrm{Tl}^{+}$are highly stable
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
The correct statements from the following are :
(A) $\mathrm{Tl}^{3+}$ is a powerful oxidising agent
(B) $\mathrm{Al}^{3+}$ does not get reduced easily
(C) Both $\mathrm{Al}^{3+}$ and $\mathrm{Tl}^{3+}$ are very stable in solution
(D) $\mathrm{Tl}^{+}$is more stable than $\mathrm{Tl}^{3+}$
(E) $\mathrm{Al}^{3+}$ and $\mathrm{Tl}^{+}$are highly stable
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
(i) True, $\mathrm{T} \ell^{+}$is more stable than $\mathrm{T} \ell^{3+}$, due to inert pair effect. So $\mathrm{T} \ell^{3+}$ is a powerful oxidising agent.
(ii) True, $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Al}^{3+/ A 1}}^0=-1.66 \mathrm{~V}$. So it is difficult to reduce $\mathrm{A} \ell^{3+}$. So $\mathrm{A} \ell^{3+}$ is highly stable.
(iii) False, as $\mathrm{T} \ell^{3+}$ is unstable
(iv) True, $\mathrm{T} \ell^{+}$is more stable than $\mathrm{T} \ell^{3+}$
(v) True, $\mathrm{A} \ell^{3+}$ and $\mathrm{T} \ell^{+}$are highly stable

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
q: In general, the property (magnitudes only) that shows an opposite trend in comparison to other properties across a period is :
question: In general, the property (magnitudes only) that shows an opposite trend in comparison to other properties across a period is :
solution: On moving Left to Right along a period. Atomic Radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>⇒</mo></math> decreases. Electronegativity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>⇒</mo></math>Increases. Electron gainenthalpy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>⇒</mo></math> Increases. Ionisation Enthalpy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>⇒</mo></math> Increases.
